topic: BOU 2017 eclipse -10.9 nT — EXP-234 P7 pre-registration rewrite (symmetric test setup + linear-coverage hedge)
change_summary: EXP-234 replaced the P7 'clean external test' paragraph inside detail_verdict_text to close c7 Hole 1 (asymmetric test setup — nine dome FSF numbers vs a vague 'consistent with Kim & Chang' competitor) and c7 Hole 2 (implicit linear-coverage assumption not flagged). The rewrite commits in text to evaluating each 2026-08-12 station at its CGM magnetic latitude (IAGA registry), using Kim & Chang (2018)'s 30°–50° response envelope as a pre-registered binary classifier (inside-band → ensemble-median signal, outside → suppressed), with an upgrade path to a smooth f(mag_lat) fit if the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available. It also hedges the coverage term as a symmetric first-order approximation and names the rigorous Sq treatment. No other WIN-010 fields changed.
our_argument_summary: WIN-010 still finds that the −10.9 nT BOU 2017 eclipse signal is fully explained by Chapman (1933) + Kim & Chang (2018) Sq physics without any dome parameter, that the dome's delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF formula imports its absolute magnitude from the 2017 observation it claims to predict, that FSF = H(r)/r IS a real dome-derived station-to-station scaling (Pearson r ≈ 0.9999), that the Kp ≈ 3 2017 baseline vs Kp<2 2026 precondition creates an asymmetric evidentiary standard, that the claim fails our independent post-hoc test (INTERMAGNET archive predated the monitor commit, no independent pre-registration), and — now with EXP-234 — that a pre-registered 2026-08-12 nine-station residual test will adjudicate dome geographic-latitude FSF against a Kim & Chang-derived magnetic-latitude classifier.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py fetches INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 minute-averaged Z-component around 17:20 UTC and compares against a hardcoded −10.9 nT target. For the 2026-08-12 prediction, a Kp<2 quiet-day gate precedes the fetch; nine per-station predicted delta_Z values (EBR −10.7, SPT −9.8, ESK −10.3, LER −9.5, HAD −12.8, NGK −6.7, SNK −5.0, CLF −10.1, COI −10.1) use a baseline × coverage × FSF formula where FSF is a published table (0.642–2.075).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: The target is a hardcoded number; the observation is fetched; the 2017 WIN therefore counts as 'hardcoded' under the classification rubric. EXP-234 made no change to this behavior — the rewrite touched only detail_verdict_text prose.
data_sources_used: INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 archive (authentic fetch for 2017 WIN)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp<2 gate for 2026 voids any future-eclipse test whenever geomagnetic activity is elevated. The 2017 baseline itself was recorded at Kp≈3 (unsettled), so the dome imposes a stricter threshold on its future tests than it met for its own WIN.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py compares a hardcoded −10.9 nT target against the fetched INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 Z-component and applies a Kp<2 gate for 2026. The hardcoded target is baked on the same commit as the fetch line. EXP-234 did not alter monitor.py or pull_data.py — only the P7 prose block inside detail_verdict_text was rewritten.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) and Kim & Chang (2018, Adv. Space Res. 61:2040–2054) quantitatively predict the BOU 2017 signal magnitude and geometry without dome parameters. Kim & Chang document Y-component increases AND X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30°–50° across a 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble. BOU sits at magnetic latitude ~48°N, inside that zone. EXP-234 did not alter this tag.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: The INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 archive was publicly available before the dome monitor.py commit; no pre-registration of the expected −10.9 nT value exists in a timestamping venue independent of the author; monitor.py hardcodes the target on the same commit as the data fetch. EXP-234 did not touch the post-hoc paragraph.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF station-to-station ratio IS derived from the dome's V12 coordinate equation r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) — Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 between claimed FSF table and independently computed H(r)/r normalized to Boulder. But FSF is a scaling RATIO between stations; the absolute magnitude at Boulder (−10.9 nT) is adopted from the 2017 observation, not derived from dome parameters. Tag remains false. EXP-234 did not alter the derives_from_dome paragraph.

holes_found.description: Component mismatch in the pre-registered comparator: the rewrite commits to 'Kim & Chang (2018)'s published 30°–50° Y-component response envelope as a pre-registered reference' (P7 sentence 4), but the dome predicts delta_Z — a Z-component magnitude — at all nine stations (explicit: delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF). Kim & Chang documented a Y-component INCREASE AND X/Z/F DECREASES at 30°–50° mag lat, so the components-matched envelope for comparing to the dome's delta_Z predictions is the Z-component (or X/Z/F) response, not the Y-component response. A capable dome defender can point to this mismatch and argue we compared apples (Y) to oranges (Z) when a directly comparable Z envelope exists in the very paper we cite. The 30°–50° zone is a Sq-current-system response zone that applies to every component, so the inside/outside-band classifier itself is still defensible — but naming the wrong component is a self-inflicted scientific sloppiness that will be cited.
holes_found.recommendation: Two-word swap in P7: replace 'Y-component response envelope' with 'Z-component response envelope' (or safer, 'X/Z/F-component response envelope' to track Kim & Chang's exact finding). Either phrasing preserves the inside-band / outside-band classifier logic while making the component match the dome's delta_Z quantity. This is a minor-edit-level follow-up EXP.

holes_found.description: The out-of-band prediction is unquantified: 'stations outside are predicted to be suppressed' attaches no numerical value to 'suppressed'. Is suppression to zero? To 20% of ensemble median? To some Kim & Chang-derived attenuation? Without a concrete number, whatever the 2026 residual turns out to be at out-of-band stations (LER ≈ 58° CGM, possibly SNK depending on station identity) can be pronounced 'suppressed enough' or 'not suppressed enough' after the fact. This re-opens precisely the post-hoc flexibility that c7 Hole 1 was meant to close on the competitor side — we named the method but left one of its two branches without a number.
holes_found.recommendation: Name a suppression factor before 2026 data arrives. Minimal acceptable fix: 'stations outside the 30°–50° band are predicted to be suppressed to ≤30% of the ensemble-median in-band signal, consistent with the Y/Z response drop-off reported in Kim & Chang (2018) Figures N' (replace N with the actual figure once verified). If the specific drop-off factor is not identifiable from the paper without fetching it, at minimum commit to a test procedure: 'suppressed' means 'observed |delta_Z| ≤ half the in-band ensemble-median value' or similar. Queue as a follow-up EXP.

holes_found.description: Binary-classifier collapse across the nine 2026-08-12 stations: the paragraph advertises 'a crude but falsifiable nine-number competitor to the dome's nine FSF numbers,' but the nine European stations cluster tightly in CGM magnetic latitude — approximately: EBR ~42°, SPT ~41°, COI ~41°, CLF ~49°, HAD ~49°, NGK ~52°, ESK ~53° (marginal/inside), LER ~58° (outside), SNK (identity ambiguous; if Sanae/South Africa it is outside, if Sodankylä it is outside, if Sonnblick it is inside). Under a binary inside/outside-band classifier this collapses to at most 2–3 unique predicted values across the nine stations (most in-band → ensemble-median; 1–3 outside → 'suppressed'). The dome's nine FSF values span ~3.2× (0.642–2.075) with no ties. When our 'nine-number competitor' is effectively a constant plus a couple of outliers, the test we are advertising is structurally much weaker than the '9-vs-9' framing implies — a dome defender can argue we rigged in favor of whichever model has more per-station variance (the dome's) and will win the residual fit almost regardless of underlying physics. EXP-234's own anticipated_objections response (objection 2) acknowledges this collapse but the acknowledgement lives in the EXP file, not in the prose a reader sees.
holes_found.recommendation: Port one sentence from EXP-234.anticipated_objections[2] into the prose. Example addition after the 'crude but falsifiable' sentence: 'Across the 2026-08-12 station set most stations sit inside the 30°–50° band, so the binary classifier predicts an approximately constant amplitude for the European core with only LER (≳58° CGM) clearly outside — this leaves the classifier predicting 2–3 unique values across nine stations, which is why the smooth-f(mag_lat) upgrade path is the preferred form if binned ensemble amplitudes become accessible.' This converts a loophole into an honest acknowledgement and preemptively neutralizes the 'rigged-for-variance' defender argument.

holes_found.description: Nonlinear-coverage parallel-drift claim is stated as exact but is only approximate: 'any nonlinear-coverage effect will drift both residuals in parallel, leaving the geographic-vs-magnetic-latitude fingerprint intact.' Ionospheric conductivity itself depends on magnetic latitude (E-region conductivity peaks at dip-equator and is modulated by magnetic-latitude structure), so nonlinear-coverage corrections can be mag-lat-dependent. A rigorous Sq treatment would therefore produce a standard-model residual that drifts with magnetic latitude under nonlinear coverage, while the dome residual drifts with geographic latitude — not parallel. The claim is first-order defensible (the dominant nonlinear-coverage effect is altitude-stratified conductivity averaging, which is geographic more than magnetic), but stating it as 'in parallel, leaving the fingerprint intact' is too strong for a pre-registration document.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften 'drift both residuals in parallel' to 'drift both residuals in broadly similar directions' or 'introduce an approximately common multiplicative error on both residuals.' Explicitly acknowledge that mag-lat-dependent conductivity structure could introduce a second-order asymmetry. This is a single-sentence hedge; queue as a follow-up micro-patch or fold into the 'suppressed' fix EXP.

holes_found.description: Persisting from c6/c7 Hole 4 (not touched by EXP-234): 'reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642–2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst' remains in P2. Independent recomputation using r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) with published IAGA station latitudes gives approximately 1/9 at <0.1%, 7/9 at <0.5%, 8/9 at <1.0%, worst ~2.16% at SPT (San Pablo). Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 (the strongest quantitative claim) is correct. The '0.1%/1.8%' numbers are overstated. EXP-234 explicitly deferred this to its own micro-patch.
holes_found.recommendation: Queue the deferred micro-patch: replace 'better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst' with 'within 0.5% at eight of nine stations and within 2.2% at the worst' (or soften to 'within approximately 1% at most stations'). Two-word swap, no conflict risk. This has been deferred across c6, c7, and now c8.

holes_found.description: Persisting from c6/c7 Hole 3 (not touched by EXP-234): P3 asserts 'any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with the dome's r·tan(θ) = H(r) convention produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form' without in-text evidence. Still a defensible soft point where a dome defender will push — 'prove it' — until we add either a footnote computation or a softer qualifier. EXP-234 explicitly deferred this.
holes_found.recommendation: Follow-up EXP adding either a one-sentence empirical footnote (e.g., refit a linear H(r) or power-law H(r) to the same sun/moon altitude calibration targets and report the resulting FSF spread) or softening 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' to 'a broad family of H(r) functions fitted to sun/moon altitude targets.' Decider should queue.

stronger_arguments.description: Kim & Chang (2018)'s 39-eclipse ensemble provides a binned amplitude table by magnetic latitude. If the raw binned values are accessible (from the paper's tables or supplementary data), we could commit now — BEFORE 2026 — to a specific per-station predicted amplitude for each of the nine stations by interpolating the binned curve at each station's CGM mag lat. This would convert the binary classifier into a nine-number competitor that actually discriminates across stations, closing both Hole 2 (unquantified 'suppressed') and Hole 3 (collapse across stations) in one move. The EXP already names this upgrade path ('If the 39-eclipse binned amplitudes become available we will instead fit a smooth f(mag_lat)'); promoting it from conditional to primary would meaningfully sharpen the pre-registration.
sources: Kim & Chang (2018), Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054 — 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET statistical study

stronger_arguments.description: A cleaner framing of the absolute-magnitude critique is available. Instead of 'training data cannot serve as test data,' say: 'the dome's formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF is dimensionally [nT] = [nT] × [dimensionless] × [dimensionless], which means the nT unit is injected at the baseline step, which is set to the 2017 BOU measurement. No dome parameter in the formula carries nanotesla units, so the absolute-magnitude dimension cannot come from dome physics — it has to come from an observation.' This makes the critique a dimensional-analysis argument rather than an epistemological one, and dimensional-analysis arguments are harder to rebut.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-234 is a genuinely good patch. Both c7 holes it targeted were real weaknesses in EXP-228's rewrite, and the new P7 does close them materially: the competitor-prediction method is now named (CGM mag lat via IAGA registry + Kim & Chang envelope classifier with upgrade path to f(mag_lat) fit), and the linear-coverage assumption is flagged symmetrically as a first-order approximation with the rigorous Sq treatment described. This turns a vague 'consistent with Kim & Chang' competitor into a pre-registered procedure that can be executed before 2026-08-12 data arrives, which was the whole point of the 'clean external test' framing.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The rewrite strengthens the 2026-onward test but does not affect the operative non-discrimination verdict. The 2017 BOU −10.9 nT is still fully explained by Chapman + Kim & Chang without dome parameters, the absolute-magnitude anchoring (2017 observation reused as 2026 baseline) still stands, the Kp asymmetry still stands, the post-hoc finding still stands, and the refraction contamination (via Section 1.8) still stands. The new P7 concerns the FUTURE 2026 test, which is outside the current verdict's scope; and even within that future test, the component-mismatch (Hole 1) and the unquantified-suppression (Hole 2) introduce new soft points that a defender can exploit.

strongest_defender_arguments: You are comparing our explicit Z-component predictions to a Y-component response envelope — you picked the wrong row of Kim & Chang's results. Either fix it (in which case you admit an error) or explain why you think Y-envelope ⟂ Z-predictions is a valid comparison.

Your 'nine-number competitor' is really a 2-to-3-number constant-plus-outliers competitor once the binary classifier is applied to the European station cluster. That is not a fair 9-vs-9 test; that is a rich per-station model vs a step function, and in the limit where our model becomes constant the test reduces to 'which single mean value do observed amplitudes cluster around.' You have pre-registered a comparison you are structurally likely to lose on variance grounds regardless of underlying physics.

Your linear-coverage 'symmetric' hedge does not fully protect against nonlinear-coverage mag-lat dependence. The standard-model side's residual drifts differently under nonlinear conductivity structure than the dome's does. Calling the residual drift 'parallel' is a first-order approximation, not a guarantee.

The 0.1%/1.8% FSF precision claim in P2 overstates the actual distribution of recomputed values (true: 0.5%/2.2%). This is a persisting accuracy defect across three review cycles and still sits in the published text.

The 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' claim in P3 is asserted without demonstration. Show me one other H(r) family that produces a comparable FSF profile from the same sun/moon calibration data, or soften the claim.
advocate_mode.how_we_respond: Holes 1, 2, 3 are accepted — they are fixable with follow-up micro-patches (component swap, suppression quantification, classifier-collapse acknowledgement). Hole 4 is a defensible softening. Holes 5 and 6 persist across cycles and should finally be batched into a consolidated cleanup EXP. None of these save the WIN's verdict — they tighten a secondary paragraph about a future test, while the operative non-discrimination argument for the 2017 observation is unchanged.

cross_win_consistency.analysis: WIN-010, WIN-025 (2024 Chile), and WIN-068 (2024 N.Am.) all invoke Chapman/Kim & Chang as the standard-physics refutation. EXP-234's sharpened pre-registration method (CGM mag lat via IAGA + Kim & Chang envelope classifier) lives only in WIN-010. If the same methodology is applicable to the WIN-068 analysis of the 2024 N.Am. eclipse (retrospective, not prospective), the detail_verdict_text of WIN-068 should reflect the same operational definition — otherwise the three-WIN cluster's refutation mechanism is inconsistently specified. EXP-234.cross_win_consistency_flag already flags this and defers it to its own EXP; the finding is consistent with the EXP's own stated propagation plan.
propagation_items: WIN-068 (2024 N.Am. eclipse): if its detail_verdict_text references a mag-lat vs geographic-lat discriminator, it should use the same IAGA+Kim & Chang envelope language. Currently deferred.
WIN-025 (2024 Chile eclipse): same consideration; deferred.

quantitative_verification.fsf_recomputation: Using r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) with published IAGA latitudes: reproduced 1/9 stations at <0.1% error, 7/9 at <0.5%, 8/9 at <1.0%, worst ~2.16% at SPT. Pearson r ≈ 0.9991 between claimed and recomputed FSF — rounds to claimed 0.9999 within rounding but only just. Replicates c6/c7 independent finding. The P2 prose overstates precision (0.1%/1.8%) by a factor of ~5 on the 'seven of nine' figure.
quantitative_verification.cgm_station_classification: Approximate CGM magnetic latitudes (from IAGA registry order-of-magnitude values, subject to sub-degree refinement with a specific CGM model year): EBR ~42°N, SPT ~41°N, COI ~41°N, CLF ~49°N, HAD ~49°N, NGK ~52°N, ESK ~53°N (marginal, inside band), LER ~58°N (outside), SNK — station identity is ambiguous from the published table; if Sanae (Antarctica, −71° geomagnetic) it is outside, if Sodankylä (~64° CGM) it is outside, if Sonnblick (~47° CGM) it is inside. Under a strict inside-30°–50°/outside binary classifier most European stations (7 or 8 of 9) fall inside the band — confirming the collapse concern in Hole 3.
quantitative_verification.kp_verification: 2017-08-21 Kp index from NOAA/GFZ records: 17:20 UTC sits in the Kp 15–18 UT interval, which in August 2017 shows Kp values roughly 2+ to 3 in the GFZ 3-hourly index. Description of 'Kp ≈ 3' in the prose is consistent with recorded values.

tldr_review.notes: Both TLDRs accurately simplify the expanded content. tldr_evidence correctly summarises the Chapman mechanism and the 2017 observation matching standard prediction. tldr_verdict correctly explains the FSF concession and the absolute-magnitude import. No factual TLDR error. No propagation fix required by EXP-234.

deeper_analysis: EXP-234 is the fourth successive refinement of WIN-010's P7 paragraph (c5 → c6 → c7 (EXP-228) → c8 (EXP-234)), and the trend is convergent: each cycle sharpens the pre-registered 2026 test by closing specific holes flagged in the prior review. This cycle's rewrite is the highest-quality of the four. The P7 is now substantively defensible as a pre-registration: method named, upgrade path named, linear-coverage assumption flagged. The three moderate holes found here (component mismatch, unquantified 'suppressed', classifier collapse) are all fixable with short follow-up patches and do not re-open the underlying scientific argument — they tighten the specific test procedure we have committed to run. The two minor persisting holes (precision overclaim, 'any H(r)' assertion) have been deferred across three cycles now and are starting to accumulate; recommend a consolidated cleanup EXP to batch them. The operative verdict — Std Model Explains — continues to hold on the strength of the 2017 Chapman/Kim & Chang explanation + absolute-magnitude anchoring + Kp asymmetry + post-hoc failure + refraction contamination; none of those legs depend on the 2026 test paragraph.

citations_verified: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) — canonical citation for the Sq-suppression eclipse mechanism; verified in prior cycles
Kim & Chang (2018, Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054) — verified in prior cycles; the Y-component increase + X/Z/F decrease finding at 30°–50° mag lat is the paper's headline result
citations_unchecked: IAGA observatory registry — the registry is authoritative for observatory IAGA codes and geographic coordinates; CGM magnetic latitudes are derived, and the specific CGM model year (IGRF epoch) used should be named in the eventual 2026 analysis note
